scenarios.name: Specific Performance of Sale Agreement

checks.detail: Specific Relief Act / specific performance cited
At least one statutory section cited
Prayer for execution of sale deed
No invented amounts (10L/50L never in facts)

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE Civil Judge (Senior Division)
AT Bengaluru

O.S. No. __________ / 20____

Anita Sharma,
Aged about 52 years,
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: Indiranagar, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

Rajesh Reddy,
Aged about 60 years,
Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: HSR Layout, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE OF CONTRACT WITH COSTS

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute this suit. The Defendant is also a person sui juris and can be sued. The agreement was executed in Bengaluru, the property is situated in Bengaluru, and the Defendant resides in Bengaluru. Hence, this Court has territorial jurisdiction under Section 20 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. The suit is filed in the Court of the lowest grade competent to try it, and the suit value is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Court under Section 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

PROCEDURAL COMPLIANCE

2. The Plaintiff states that the suit is subject to the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 due to the specified value. The Plaintiff confirms that pre-institution mediation under Section 12A has been completed or is exempted as per {{COMMERCIAL_COURTS_COMPLIANCE_STATUS}}.

LIMITATION

3. The suit is within limitation under Article 54 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, the period prescribed being three years from the date on which the contract was broken. The suit is filed well within the prescribed period.

FACTS OF THE AGREEMENT

4. On 10.01.2024, the Plaintiff entered into an Agreement to Sell with the Defendant for purchase of residential flat No. 302, 3rd Floor, Sunshine Apartments, Koramangala, Bengaluru, measuring 1200 sq ft. The total consideration agreed was Rs. 85,00,000/-.
5. On the date of the agreement, i.e., 10.01.2024, the Plaintiff paid an advance amount of Rs. 25,00,000/- to the Defendant via RTGS (UTR: HDFC20240110987654). The balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/- was agreed to be paid on or before 10.07.2024 against execution of the sale deed.
6. The terms of the agreement are recorded in the Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024, marked as Annexure A. The payment of advance is evidenced by the bank receipt marked as Annexure B.

PLAINTIFF'S READINESS AND WILLINGNESS

7. The Plaintiff has always been and continues to be ready and willing to perform her part of the contract. The Plaintiff has arranged the balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/- and is ready to pay the same against execution of the sale deed.
8. The Plaintiff has prepared all necessary documents and is ready to execute the sale deed as per the terms of the agreement. The Plaintiff repeatedly offered to perform her obligation, demonstrating continuous readiness and willingness to complete the transaction.

DEFENDANT'S REFUSAL TO PERFORM

9. On 05.06.2024, the Defendant sent a letter refusing to execute the sale deed, claiming he had received a higher offer for the property. This letter is marked as Annexure C.
10. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 20.06.2024 calling upon the Defendant to execute the sale deed as per the agreement. The legal notice with postal receipt is marked as Annexure D. The Defendant refused to comply with the notice and failed to execute the sale deed.

LEGAL BASIS

11. The Plaintiff is entitled to specific performance under Section 10 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963, as the contract is in writing and signed, and is enforceable subject to the provisions regarding contracts not specifically enforceable. The contract is for transfer of immovable property and is capable of being supervised by the Court.
12. The Plaintiff has continuously performed and ready to perform her obligations as required under Section 16 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963. The Plaintiff pleads specific factual acts showing continuous readiness, including arranging the balance consideration and repeated offers to perform.
13. Time is not of the essence of the contract for immovable property unless expressly stipulated, and the Plaintiff is entitled to perform within a reasonable time. Under Section 53A of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882, the Plaintiff is protected against eviction as part performance has been initiated through payment of advance and readiness to perform.
14. The Plaintiff is also entitled to compensation under Section 21 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 and possession under Section 22 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 if granted. The contract is valid under Section 10 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872.

CAUSE OF ACTION

15. The cause of action first arose on 05.06.2024 when the Defendant refused to execute the sale deed despite the Plaintiff's readiness and willingness to perform.
16. The cause of action subsequently arose on 20.06.2024 when the Defendant failed to comply with the legal notice issued by the Plaintiff. The suit is filed within the limitation period governed by Article 54 of the Limitation Act, 1963.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

17. The total suit valuation is Rs. 85,00,000/- (Rupees Eighty Five Lakhs only) for the purpose of jurisdiction and court fee.
18. The court fee amount paid is {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} computed under the {{COURT_FEE_ACT_NAME}}. The plaint is properly stamped and tendered with the requisite court fee.

PRAYER

19. In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:
(a) Pass a decree for specific performance of the Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024 directing the Defendant to execute the sale deed in favour of the Plaintiff upon payment of the balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/-;
(b) Direct the Defendant to deliver vacant and peaceful possession of the suit property to the Plaintiff if not already in possession;
(c) In the alternative, award compensation under Section 21 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 in lieu of or in addition to specific performance, at such amount as this Hon'ble Court deems fit;
(d) Award pre-suit interest if contract, usage, or substantive law permits, pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC, and future interest under Section 34 CPC as this Hon'ble Court deems fit;
(e) Award costs of the suit to the Plaintiff;
(f) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

SCHEDULE OF SUIT PROPERTY

{{SCHEDULE_OF_SUIT_PROPERTY -- Address, Survey/Plot No., Extent/Area, Boundaries (East/West/North/South)}}

LIST OF DOCUMENTS

Annexure A — Original agreement dated 10.01.2024

Annexure B — RTGS receipt (UTR: HDFC20240110987654)

Annexure C — Defendant's refusal letter dated 05.06.2024

Annexure D — Legal notice dated 20.06.2024 with postal receipt

VERIFICATION

Verified at {{CITY}} on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 19 of this plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and no material facts have been concealed therefrom, and that I have not filed any other suit, petition, or proceeding in any court or before any authority in respect of the same subject matter and cause of action.

DEPONENT

{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}

PLAINTIFF

                                    THROUGH:
                                    {{ADVOCATE_NAME}}
                                    Advocate for the Plaintiff
                                    Enrollment No. {{ENROLLMENT_NO}}
                                    {{ADVOCATE_ADDRESS}}
                                    Mobile: {{ADVOCATE_PHONE}}
                                    Email: {{ADVOCATE_EMAIL}}

scenarios.name: Breach of Contract — Damages (Business)

checks.detail: Total claim Rs.32L or breakdown stated

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE COMMERCIAL COURT (COMMERCIAL DIVISION)
AT BENGALURU

C.S. No. __________ / 20____

TechSoft Solutions Pvt Ltd,
A Company Registered under the Companies Act, 1956/2013,
Having its Registered Office at: MG Road, Bengaluru,
Represented by its Director Priya Menon, Aged 40 years.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

CloudBridge Systems LLP,
A Limited Liability Partnership,
Having its Office at: Whitefield, Bengaluru,
Represented by its Designated Partner Vikram Singh, Aged 48 years.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF DAMAGES FOR BREACH OF CONTRACT UNDER SECTION 73 OF THE INDIAN CONTRACT ACT, 1872

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

COMMERCIAL COURT MAINTAINABILITY

1. The subject matter of the present suit arises out of a commercial dispute within the meaning of Section 2(1)(c) of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015. The suit is valued above the specified value of Rs. 32,00,000/- as prescribed under Section 12 of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, and is therefore maintainable before this Hon'ble Commercial Court.

JURISDICTION

2. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is a person sui juris and can be sued in its own name as a limited liability partnership.

3. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction under Section 20 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, as the Defendant resides and carries on business at Whitefield, Bengaluru, and the contract was executed and was to be performed within Bengaluru. The cause of action arose within the jurisdiction of this Court.

4. The suit is filed in the Court of the lowest grade competent to try it, and the suit value falls within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Hon'ble Commercial Court. The dispute is a commercial dispute of specified value as defined under the Commercial Courts Act, 2015.

LIMITATION

5. The suit is within limitation under Article 55 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, the period prescribed being three years from the date on which the contract was broken. The suit is filed well within the prescribed period.

SECTION 12A COMPLIANCE

6. The Plaintiff confirms that pre-institution mediation under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 was conducted on [DATE] before [MEDIATION AUTHORITY] and resulted in [OUTCOME]. Certificate of mediation is annexed. OR The Plaintiff seeks urgent interim relief and is exempt from pre-institution mediation requirement under Section 12A proviso.

FACTS OF THE CASE

7. On 01.02.2024, the Plaintiff entered into a written Software Development Agreement with the Defendant for the development of an ERP system, with a total contract value of Rs. 45,00,000/-. The agreement was executed at Bengaluru, and the performance was due to be completed at the Plaintiff's premises located at MG Road, Bengaluru. A copy of the Software Development Agreement is annexed hereto as Annexure A.

8. Pursuant to the terms of the Agreement, the Plaintiff paid the first milestone payment of Rs. 20,00,000/- to the Defendant on 01.02.2024 via NEFT bearing UTR No. ICIC20240201456789. The Plaintiff has fully performed its financial obligation under the contract to facilitate the commencement of work by the Defendant. A copy of the NEFT receipt is annexed hereto as Annexure B.

9. The Defendant was contractually bound to deliver the first module of the ERP system by 01.05.2024. Both parties are competent to contract, with the Plaintiff being a company registered under the Companies Act and the Defendant being a limited liability partnership, both represented by their respective authorised signatories. The Plaintiff relies on the correspondence and payment records to demonstrate its readiness and performance.

BREACH OF CONTRACT

10. The Defendant failed to deliver the first module of the ERP system by the agreed deadline of 01.05.2024, thereby committing a material breach of the essential terms of the Agreement. The non-delivery occurred despite the Plaintiff having made the requisite advance payment and provided all necessary inputs.

11. On 15.06.2024, the Defendant unilaterally abandoned the project without completing the deliverables and without refunding the advance payment made by the Plaintiff. Email correspondence between the parties confirms the abandonment of the project by the Defendant. A copy of the email correspondence is annexed hereto as Annexure C.

12. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice to the Defendant dated 01.07.2024 demanding refund of the advance and compensation for losses suffered. The Defendant has failed to respond favourably or return the advance amount despite the notice. A copy of the legal notice is annexed hereto as Annexure E.

LOSS AND DAMAGE

13. The Plaintiff has suffered an actual loss of Rs. 20,00,000/- being the advance payment made to the Defendant which remains unrefunded. This amount represents the direct financial expenditure incurred by the Plaintiff under the contract.

14. The Plaintiff further suffered consequential damages amounting to Rs. 12,00,000/- due to the delay in operations caused by the non-delivery of the ERP system. This loss was within the contemplation of both parties at the time of contract formation as the ERP system was critical for the Plaintiff's business operations.

15. The causal link between the Defendant's breach and the loss is established by the fact that the operational delay occurred solely due to the non-delivery of the software module by the agreed deadline. The loss naturally arises in the usual course from such a breach of a development agreement.

16. The Plaintiff engaged an auditor to assess the loss and took reasonable steps to mitigate the impact of the breach. The loss assessment report quantifies the damages and confirms that the Plaintiff acted reasonably to minimize the loss. A copy of the loss assessment report by the auditor is annexed hereto as Annexure D.

LEGAL BASIS

17. The Plaintiff is entitled to compensation for the breach of contract under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872. The Plaintiff claims compensation for any loss or damage caused by the breach which naturally arose in the usual course of things from the breach itself.

18. Time was of the essence in the contract regarding the delivery deadline of 01.05.2024. The Plaintiff invokes Section 55 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, as the failure to perform at the fixed time has rendered the contract voidable at the option of the Plaintiff.

19. The Plaintiff pleads that no subsequent agreement replaced or discharged the original contract, and there is no novation or accord and satisfaction between the parties. The original contract remains binding and the breach stands uncompensated.

20. The Plaintiff specifically pleads that it took steps to minimise the loss after the breach, addressing any potential defence regarding failure to mitigate. The Plaintiff is entitled to the relief sought under the cited provisions as the Defendant's breach is established and uncompensated.

CAUSE OF ACTION

21. The cause of action first arose on 01.05.2024 when the Defendant failed to deliver the first module by the agreed deadline, and subsequently on 15.06.2024 when the Defendant abandoned the project.

22. The suit is governed by Article 55 of the Limitation Act, 1963, which prescribes a limitation period of three years from the date the contract is broken. The present suit is filed within the prescribed limitation period.

23. The breach is a specific event based on the missed deadline and abandonment, and not a continuing breach. The right to sue accrued on the dates mentioned above and remains subsisting within the limitation period.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

24. The total suit valuation is Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only) for the purpose of jurisdiction and court fee. This amount comprises the refund of advance and consequent
